4.note: By faith Abel brought God a better offering than Cain did. By faith he still speaks, even though he is dead — the NT retrospect on the Cain/Abel offering reads Abel's as an act of faith and Cain's as faithless. The distinction Genesis draws (fat portions from the firstborn vs. some of the fruits without qualification) becomes the canonical grounding for the sacrificial principle that faith determines the quality of the gift.

All the fat is the LORD's; this is a lasting ordinance for the generations to come — the Levitical law that the fat belongs to YHWH echoes Abel's prototype offering of fat portions from the firstborn. Abel's instinct to give the best and richest portion anticipates the sacrificial code that institutionalizes the same principle centuries later.

Not like Cain who was of the evil one and murdered his brother — Cain becomes the NT type of the world's murderous hatred of the righteous; the community that faces hatred for righteousness stands where Abel stood

7.note: Do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its evil desires — Paul's imperative against sin reigning (basileuo) maps onto the divine warning to Cain that sin is crouching at the door and desires to have you, but you must rule over it. Both texts locate the decisive battle in the will's response to the personified, desire-language of sin seeking mastery over the human agent.

Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour — the image of sin crouching (Hebrew: robets, the posture of a predator lying in wait) is the same as Peter's prowling lion. Both texts use predatory-animal imagery to describe the posture of moral danger waiting to strike.

8.note: Do not be like Cain, who belonged to the evil one and murdered his brother. And why did he murder him? Because his own actions were evil and his brother's were righteous — the NT canonical reading identifies Cain's murder as flowing from spiritual allegiance (evil one) and the contrast between evil works and righteous works. The Genesis narrative of the rejected offering provides the backstory that 1 John reads as paradigmatic for sibling-hatred in the community.

Woe to them! They have taken the way of Cain — Jude uses the way of Cain as one of three OT archetypes for the false teachers (Cain, Balaam, Korah). The way of Cain is the path of offering without faith, of envy that becomes violence, and of the refusal to hear divine correction before the sin crouches and springs.

10.note: To Jesus the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel — the blood of Abel crying out from the ground is the canonical precedent that Hebrews invokes to describe the blood of Christ. Abel's blood spoke accusation and demanded justice; Christ's blood speaks forgiveness and provides atonement. Both bloods speak from the ground, but with opposite valence.

How long, Sovereign Lord, holy and true, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood? — the martyrs under the altar in Revelation cry out in the same category as Abel's blood crying from the ground: murdered righteous blood demanding divine vindication. The blood-cry motif of Gen 4:10 becomes the paradigm for all martyrdom-vindication language in the canon.

14.note: Do not cast me from your presence or take your Holy Spirit from me — David's plea not to be driven from the divine presence uses the same fear Cain voices in Gen 4:14: I will be hidden from your presence. Both texts treat exclusion from the divine presence as the ultimate punishment; David prays not to receive what Cain suffered.

23.note: I tell you, not seven times, but seventy-seven times — Jesus's instruction to forgive 77 times (or 70 times 7) is a deliberate inversion of Lamech's boast: if Cain is avenged seven times, then Lamech seventy-seven times. The Lamech escalation (from 7x divine protection of Cain to 77x self-vindication) represents the metastasis of violence; Jesus sets the anti-Lamech number as the pattern for the new community.

26.note: Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved — Peter's Pentecost citation of Joel 2:32 stands on the foundation laid in Gen 4:26: at that time people began to call on the name of the LORD. Seth's line, not Cain's, is the line that worships, and the calling on the divine name that begins here runs through Joel's prophecy to Peter's proclamation.

1.note: This is the genealogy (biblios geneseos) of Jesus the Messiah the son of David, the son of Abraham — Matthew's opening formula is the Greek rendering of Genesis's sefer toledot adam (this is the written account of Adam's family line). By deliberately using the same phrase, Matthew signals that his book is a new Genesis: the genealogy of Jesus recapitulates and surpasses the genealogy of Adam.

The son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God — Luke's genealogy traces Jesus back through Genesis 5 to Adam and ultimately to God. The sefer toledot adam that begins in Gen 5:1 culminates in Luke's genealogy by identifying Adam as son of God, making Jesus the last Adam who restores what the first Adam lost.

3.note: Just as we have borne the image of the earthly man, so shall we bear the image of the heavenly man — Gen 5:3 establishes that Seth was born in Adam's own likeness and image. The transmission of the image through the Adamic line (Gen 5:3) is the context for Paul's contrast: we inherit the fallen image of the earthly Adam, but resurrection transforms us into the image of the heavenly Christ.

24.note: By faith Enoch was taken from this life, so that he did not experience death; he could not be found, because God had taken him away — Hebrews reads Enoch's translation as an act of faith: walking faithfully with God (Gen 5:22, 24) is interpreted as a faith-condition that resulted in divine approval and bodily transformation. Enoch's translation becomes a type of resurrection hope.

Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied about them: See, the Lord is coming with thousands upon thousands of his holy ones to judge everyone — Jude cites a saying attributed to Enoch (from 1 Enoch 1:9) as genuine prophecy. The canonical Enoch who walked with God and was taken becomes in Second Temple tradition the exemplary prophet who foresaw the final judgment.

2.note: God did not spare angels when they sinned, but sent them to hell, putting them in chains of darkness to be held for judgment — 2 Peter reads the sons of God who married the daughters of humans (Gen 6:2) as angelic beings who transgressed the boundary between heavenly and human realms. The Noahic judgment narrative is partially explained by this supernatural transgression preceding it.

The angels who did not keep their positions of authority but abandoned their proper dwelling — these he has kept in darkness, bound with everlasting chains for judgment on the great Day — Jude, like 2 Peter, reads Gen 6:2 as describing supernatural beings (sons of God = angels) who violated their created order. The sons of God narrative is the genesis of a canonical tradition about fallen angelic beings.

5.note: The heart is deceitful above all things and beyond cure. Who can understand it? — Jeremiah's diagnosis of the human heart's radical corruption is the prophetic articulation of what Gen 6:5 narrates: every inclination of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil all the time. The yetzer ha-ra (evil inclination) that fills Genesis 6 becomes the theological premise for the prophetic call to repentance.

There is no one righteous, not even one; there is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God — Paul's catena of OT quotations in Romans 3 making the case for universal sinfulness stands on the foundation of Gen 6:5. The totality language (every inclination... only evil... all the time) is the narrative form of the doctrinal universal that Paul argues theologically.

8.note: You have said, I know you by name and you have found favor with me — the first use of chen (grace/favor) in Scripture occurs here: Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD. The same vocabulary of finding favor (matsa chen) recurs when Moses pleads for divine presence; the grace vocabulary of the canon originates in Noah's exceptional standing before YHWH in a generation that was wholly corrupt.

9.note: By faith Noah, when warned about things not yet seen, in holy fear built an ark to save his family. By his faith he condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness that is in keeping with faith — Hebrews reads Noah's righteous and blameless walk (Gen 6:9) through the lens of faith: the trust that acted on divine warning about what had never happened before is the faith that both saved his family and indicted the unbelieving generation.

Even if these three men — Noah, Daniel and Job — were in it, they could save only themselves by their righteousness — Ezekiel's triad of supremely righteous men places Noah first and uses his righteousness as the canonical standard for individual integrity. Gen 6:9's description of Noah as righteous and blameless is the text Ezekiel is building on when he uses Noah as one of the three paradigms of righteousness.

18.note: I now establish my covenant with you and with your descendants after you — the first use of berit (covenant) in Scripture occurs in Gen 6:18 as God announces what he will establish with Noah. The covenant vocabulary that structures the entire canon (Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, new covenant) has its lexical origin in this pre-flood promise to Noah.

The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make a new covenant with the people of Israel and Judah — Jeremiah's new covenant promise is intelligible against the full covenant trajectory that begins with Noah in Gen 6:18. The covenant that preserves Noah through judgment anticipates the covenant that will preserve the new humanity through eschatological renewal.

1.note: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God... all things were made through him.' John's Gospel opens with the same words as Genesis 1:1 (en arche / bereshit) deliberately, identifying the Word (Logos) as the divine agent of the original creation. Genesis 1:1 is the seed John 1:1-3 germinates — the same 'beginning,' the same creative activity, now personalized in Christ

'All things were created through him and for him... in him all things hold together.' Paul's cosmic Christology reads the Genesis 1 creation as mediated through the Son; the 'all things' of v1 (heavens and earth) is the same 'all things' now attributed to Christ's agency as the one through whom and for whom creation exists

'Through whom he also made the ages (tous aionas).' Hebrews describes the Son as the agent of creation, echoing Gen 1:1 — the 'beginning' of creation is the beginning of the Son's creative work. The plural 'ages' expands Genesis' temporal frame to include all epochs of history as created through the Son

In the beginning God created — John 1:1 deliberately echoes Gen 1:1 (en arche = bereshit); what God did in Gen 1:1 through the word, the Word (Logos) was present to do; the new creation of the gospel is narrated in the same opening cadence as the first creation
The first heaven and earth (Gen 1:1) will pass away and be replaced by new heavens and new earth; Revelation's new creation is the eschatological bookend to Genesis's original creation

2.note: 'The Spirit of God descending like a dove' at Jesus's baptism echoes the Spirit of God hovering (merachefet) over the primordial waters in Gen 1:2. Matthew's dove-descending is a new-creation signal: just as the Spirit hovered over the formless chaos before creation began, the Spirit's descent at baptism marks the beginning of the new creation in Christ

Jesus breathes on the disciples and says 'Receive the Holy Spirit' — a deliberate echo of both Gen 1:2 (Spirit over the waters) and Gen 2:7 (YHWH breathing life into Adam). John's new-creation Pentecost re-enacts the original creation/animation: as the Spirit hovered over the pre-creation void, the risen Christ breathes the eschatological life of the Spirit into the new-creation community

3.note: 'God who said, Let light shine out of darkness, has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.' Paul reads the Genesis 1:3 creation-light command as a type fulfilled in conversion: as the primal darkness was pierced by the divine word 'let there be light,' so the spiritual darkness of the unregenerate heart is pierced by the gospel of Christ's glory

'In him was life, and that life was the light of all mankind. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.' John's light-in-darkness language is structurally continuous with Gen 1:3-4: the Word who was the agent of the original light-creation is himself the light in the Johannine prologue; the separation of light from darkness in Genesis is now the cosmic drama of the Word's coming into the world

26.note: 'He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation.' Paul's Christological application of the imago dei: Christ is not merely made 'in' the image but IS the image (eikon) of which Genesis 1:26-27 speaks. The human creature made in the divine image is a derivative of the Son who is the original image; Christ is the archetype, humanity the ectype

'The god of this age has blinded the minds of unbelievers, so that they cannot see the light of the gospel that displays the glory of Christ, who is the image of God.' Paul's identification of Christ as the image of God (eikon tou theou) in the evangelistic context applies the Genesis 1:26-27 language to Christ: the glory the Fall obscured is the glory that shines in Christ's face

'And just as we have borne the image of the earthly man, so shall we bear the image of the heavenly man.' The eschatological restoration of the imago dei: the image of the dusty Adam (the creational image now marred by sin) will be replaced by the image of the heavenly Christ. Genesis 1:26-27 establishes the destiny; 1 Cor 15:49 announces its eschatological recovery

Made in the image of God (imago Dei) — humanity's creation in the divine image is the basis for the new-creation renewal in Colossians: the new self is being renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator; the image lost in the Fall is restored in Christ

Predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son — God's original purpose in creating humanity in his image (Gen 1:26) is eschatologically completed in conformity to the image of Christ, the perfect image-bearer

27.note: 'Have you not read that he who created them from the beginning made them male and female?' Jesus cites Gen 1:27 in his divorce teaching, appealing to the creational design ('from the beginning') as the norm that supersedes Mosaic concession. The male-female binary of Genesis 1:27 is the hermeneutical starting point for Jesus's teaching on marriage's permanence

Mark's parallel account of Jesus's divorce teaching also cites Gen 1:27 explicitly. Both Matthew and Mark record Jesus grounding the permanence of marriage in the creation ordinance — the two sexes as a divine design feature, not a cultural accident

'With the tongue we curse human beings, who have been made in God's likeness.' James grounds the moral prohibition against cursing in the imago dei of Gen 1:26-27 — image-bearers are not to be cursed because cursing an image-bearer attacks the one whose image it is

28.note: 'You put everything under their feet' — the author of Hebrews cites Psalm 8:6 but reads it against the Genesis 1:28 commission to subdue and rule. The Psalm-citation is applied to Christ: what Genesis 1:28 commissioned (humanity ruling creation) and what Psalm 8 celebrated (human dignity) is now fulfilled in the exalted Christ who has all things under his feet — but the 'not yet' of universal subjection awaits

2.note: 'He has spoken in a certain place about the seventh day in these words: On the seventh day God rested from all his works.' Hebrews quotes Gen 2:2 as the foundation for its extended argument about the 'sabbath rest' that remains for the people of God. The divine rest of the seventh day is both the prototype of the promised land-rest and the anticipation of the eschatological rest that believers enter through faith in Christ

'Do not let anyone judge you by what you eat or drink, or with regard to a religious festival, a New Moon celebration or a Sabbath day. These are a shadow of the things that were to come; the reality, however, is found in Christ.' The sabbath of Gen 2:2-3 is here called a 'shadow' — a forward-pointing provisional form — whose substance/body (soma) is Christ. Genesis 2:2's rest is the shadow; Christ is the reality it was pointing to

7.note: 'So it is written: The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam, a life-giving spirit.' Paul quotes Gen 2:7 LXX exactly ('the man became a living being' = egeneto ho anthropos eis psychen zosan) as the protological basis for the Adam-Christ typology. The 'first Adam' of Gen 2:7 receives life from YHWH; the 'last Adam' gives life eschatologically. Paul reads Gen 2:7 as the thesis statement of the creation-order that Christ fulfills and transcends

'The first man was of the dust of the earth; the second man is of heaven.' Paul's Adam-from-dust language directly depends on Gen 2:7 (aphar min-ha'adamah = dust from the ground). The earthly origin of the first man — formed from soil — contrasts with the heavenly origin of Christ; the material of creation defines the nature of the fall and the nature of the redemption

Jesus breathes on the disciples and says 'Receive the Holy Spirit' — a deliberate recall of YHWH breathing life (nishmat chayyim) into Adam in Gen 2:7. As the original creation involved divine breath animating dust into a living being, the new creation involves the risen Christ breathing eschatological life into the community. John's Pentecost is a Gen 2:7 re-enactment

YHWH formed man from the dust and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and man became a living being — Paul quotes Gen 2:7 LXX exactly (psuche zosa) to contrast the first Adam (living soul) with the last Adam (life-giving spirit); the first creation's humanity is the baseline against which the new creation's humanity is measured

9.note: 'To the one who is victorious, I will give the right to eat from the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God.' The tree of life in the garden of Eden (Gen 2:9) reappears in the eschatological paradise as the reward for persevering faith. What was denied to Adam after the Fall (blocked by cherubim in 3:24) is restored to the overcomer in Christ

'On each side of the river stood the tree of life, bearing twelve crops of fruit, yielding its fruit every month.' The New Jerusalem's tree of life is the Eden tree of Gen 2:9 restored and magnified: what was one tree in one garden becomes an abundant grove accessible to all nations. The end of Revelation consciously mirrors and surpasses the beginning of Genesis

17.note: 'Just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned.' Paul's Adam-typology is grounded in the Gen 2:17 command — 'in the day you eat of it you will surely die.' The entrance of death into the world traces back to this transgressed command; the one man through whom death came is the man who violated the Gen 2:17 prohibition

'In Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive.' The universal scope of death-in-Adam derives from Gen 2:17's threatened consequence: the death-sentence pronounced on one man (Adam) became the death-reality for all humanity. The Corinthian passage makes explicit what Genesis implies: all are 'in Adam,' all inherit the consequence of Gen 2:17's violated prohibition

23.note: 'For no one ever hated their own body, but they feed and care for their body, just as Christ does the church — for we are members of his body.' Paul's argument develops from Gen 2:23 (woman taken from man's body) and 2:24 (they become one flesh): the church-as-body-of-Christ mirrors the woman-taken-from-man's-body, and Christ's care for the church mirrors a man's care for his own body/flesh

'For man did not come from woman, but woman from man.' Paul's argument about head coverings draws on the creation sequence of Gen 2:21-23 — the woman was taken from the man, not vice versa. The direction of creational derivation (woman from man) is Paul's warrant for the theological asymmetry he is establishing in the Corinthian context

24.note: Jesus cites Gen 2:24 in the divorce controversy: 'A man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh.' Jesus attributes the Gen 2:24 statement to God's design ('he who created them...said'), making the creation narrative the normative standard for marriage — what God joined together in Eden, humans must not separate

'For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh. This is a profound mystery — but I am talking about Christ and the church.' Paul quotes Gen 2:24 and then declares that its ultimate referent is the Christ-church union. The mystery hidden in the creation narrative of Gen 2 is the eschatological union of Christ and his people

'Do you not know that he who unites himself with a prostitute is one with her in body? For it is said, The two will become one flesh.' Paul cites Gen 2:24 to argue that sexual union creates a real metaphysical bond, not merely a casual physical contact. The one-flesh union of Gen 2:24 is Paul's ground for the seriousness of sexual immorality: it unites believers with whoever they sleep with

A man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife and they shall become one flesh — Paul quotes Gen 2:24 and calls it a profound mystery (mysterion mega) referring to Christ and the church; the one-flesh union of marriage is the type of Christ's union with the church

1.note: 'I am afraid that just as Eve was deceived by the serpent's cunning, your minds may somehow be led astray from your sincere and pure devotion to Christ.' Paul uses the Gen 3:1-6 serpent/Eve encounter as the paradigm for doctrinal deception — the serpent's craft (Gen 3:1 arum) is the model for the false apostles' deception; the Corinthians are in danger of the same pattern of corrupted thinking that destroyed Eve's single-minded fidelity

'The great dragon was hurled down — that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray.' Revelation's cosmic drama identifies the Genesis 3 serpent with the dragon, the devil, and Satan — the explicit identification that Genesis leaves implicit. The 'ancient serpent' designation (also Rev 20:2) traces the adversary's history back to the Eden encounter

'He was a murderer from the beginning, not holding to the truth, for there is no truth in him.' Jesus's characterization of the devil as 'a murderer from the beginning' and a liar refers back to Gen 3:1-6 — the serpent's first recorded speech is a lie ('you will not certainly die'); the murder-from-the-beginning echoes John 8:44's connection with the Fall as the origin of the devil's homicidal career

6.note: 'And Adam was not the one deceived; it was the woman who was deceived and became a sinner.' Paul's pastoral argument regarding teaching authority draws on the Fall sequence of Gen 3:6 — Eve was deceived and ate; Adam was not deceived but sinned knowingly. The order and manner of the Fall (serpent → Eve → Adam) is Paul's argument; he distinguishes the deception of Eve from Adam's sin

'For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous.' The disobedience Paul traces to 'the one man' (Adam) is the deliberate act of eating in Gen 3:6: 'he ate it.' The Adam-Christ parallel hinges on this moment of one man's decisive choice determining the destiny of many

15.note: 'But when the set time had fully come, God sent his Son, born of a woman, born under the law.' Paul's 'born of a woman' echoes the Gen 3:15 'offspring of the woman' — the promised seed-of-the-woman who will crush the serpent is specifically born of woman (not of man alone), and Jesus's birth fulfills the proto-evangelion. The woman's seed becomes the Seed born of Mary

'The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet.' Paul's closing promise is a direct echo of Gen 3:15 ('he will crush your head') — the same crushing action, the same adversary. What God promised in Eden (the woman's seed crushing the serpent's head) is now promised to the Roman church: the eschatological crushing of Satan is imminent, mediated through the community of Christ

'The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the devil's work.' 1 John interprets the Incarnation teleologically against the Gen 3:15 background: the Son's appearance was specifically purposed to undo what the serpent's work began in Eden. The 'work of the devil' (deception, sin, death) introduced in Genesis 3 is the problem Christ's coming was designed to solve

'Since the children have flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death — that is, the devil.' Hebrews frames the Incarnation as the necessary step for defeating the death-power that traces back to Gen 3:15's adversary; the crushing of the serpent required the Seed to share human mortality, taking the serpent's strike on himself to accomplish the crushing blow

That ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, who deceives the whole world — Revelation's identification of the serpent as Satan retroactively clarifies who the Gen 3 serpent was; the Gen 3:15 enmity reaches its climax in Rev 12's cosmic conflict

17.note: 'For the creation was subjected to frustration (mataioteti), not by its own choice, but by the will of the one who subjected it, in hope... the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth.' Paul's creation-frustration/groaning directly echoes Gen 3:17-19 — the ground cursed because of Adam, thorns and thistles, sweated labor, death-return-to-dust. The cosmic curse of Gen 3:17-19 is Paul's datum for creation's present subjection to futility

'Sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people.' The death that entered through the one man (Adam) is the death threatened in Gen 2:17 and fulfilled in Gen 3:19 ('for dust you are and to dust you shall return'). Gen 3:17-19 is the empirical confirmation of Gen 2:17's warning; Rom 5:12 is the theological analysis of the universal consequence

19.note: 'The first man was of the dust of the earth.' Paul's Adam-from-dust language (1 Cor 15:45-47) draws simultaneously on Gen 2:7 (formed from dust) and Gen 3:19 ('for dust you are and to dust you shall return'). The dusty origin and the dusty destiny belong together in Paul's argument: the Christ who comes from heaven transforms the dust-destiny that the Adam-narrative establishes

'In Adam all die' — the universal death-in-Adam derives from Gen 3:19's 'you will return to the ground' applied to all Adam's descendants. The death-sentence of 3:19 is the grounding claim for Paul's 'in Adam all die'; the one man's death-doom becomes the common lot of humanity

21.note: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.' The first animal deaths in the biblical narrative occur in Gen 3:21 — God kills animals to provide skin-coverings for the sinful pair. The first blood is shed not by humans but by God, to cover human shame. Hebrews' principle that forgiveness requires blood-shedding is illustrated in germ by this first act of sacrificial covering; the pattern established in Eden runs through the Levitical sacrificial system to Christ

'They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.' The garments of skin God provided in Gen 3:21 (covering the shame of sin) are the distant type of the white robes of the redeemed in Revelation. Both involve divine provision of covering for the shame of sin; both involve death (animal/Lamb) as the means of covering. The trajectory from skin-garments to white robes passes through the atoning death of Christ

22.note: 'Blessed are those who wash their robes, that they may have the right to the tree of life and may go through the gates into the city.' Gen 3:22-24 narrates the expulsion from the garden and the blocking of access to the tree of life. Revelation 22 narrates the reversal: access to the tree of life is restored to those washed in the Lamb's blood. What the cherubim blocked in Genesis, the Lamb's atoning work reopens in Revelation

'We have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way opened for us through the curtain.' The curtain/veil blocking access to the divine presence in the tabernacle corresponds to the cherubim and flaming sword blocking re-entry to Eden in Gen 3:24. Hebrews' announcement that the way into the Most Holy Place is now open through Christ's blood is the reversal of the Gen 3:24 exclusion

24.note: 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' After the Fall, the way to the tree of life was blocked (Gen 3:24 — the cherubim and flaming sword). Jesus's 'I am the way' declaration implicitly answers Gen 3:24's blocked path: the return to the life of God, of which the tree of life was the symbol, is now opened through Christ who is himself the way

1.note: By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go out to a place that he was to receive as an inheritance — the Abrahamic call and response (lekh-lekha) is interpreted in Hebrews as the paradigmatic faith-obedience: he went out, not knowing where he was going

Stephen's speech begins with the God of glory appearing to Abraham in Mesopotamia before he lived in Haran; the Abrahamic call inaugurates salvation history and is the foundational moment Stephen uses to show Israel's God as the God who works outside the land

3.note: In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed — Paul reads the Abrahamic promise as the preaching of the gospel in advance: the Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith and preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham; the blessing to all nations is fulfilled in the Gentile mission of Christ

6.note: Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness — Paul's foundational text for justification by faith; Gen 15:6 is cited as the decisive proof that righteousness before God has always been by faith, not by works (since circumcision came later in ch. 17)

Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness — Paul cites Gen 15:6 to show that the blessing of Abraham comes to the Gentiles through faith, not through Torah-observance; the Galatian churches participate in the Abrahamic faith-righteousness

8.note: God will provide for himself the lamb — the Aqedah (binding of Isaac): Abraham's willingness to sacrifice his only son is the OT type of God the Father giving his Son; 'God himself will provide the lamb' is fulfilled when God provides Christ as the Passover Lamb

By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac, and he who had received the promises was in the act of offering up his only son, of whom it was said: Through Isaac shall your offspring be named — he considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead; the Aqedah is interpreted as a resurrection-typology: Isaac's deliverance from death prefigured Christ's resurrection

12.note: Jacob's ladder with angels ascending and descending — Jesus applies the Jacob's ladder vision to himself: you will see heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man; Christ is the ladder/stairway between heaven and earth, the meeting point of divine and human

28.note: Joseph sold by his brothers for twenty pieces of silver — Stephen's speech identifies the Joseph narrative as a type of the rejection of Christ by his own people; as the patriarchs were jealous of Joseph and sold him, so they rejected Christ

10.note: The scepter shall not depart from Judah nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until tribute comes to him — the Lion of the tribe of Judah in Revelation is the fulfillment of Jacob's blessing on Judah; the royal authority of Gen 49:10 is Christ's eschatological sovereignty
